advantages: Precision and recall decompose error types (false positives vs false negatives) that accuracy collapses on imbalanced data.
F1 provides a single balanced score when neither false alarms nor missed positives should dominate.
Metrics are interpretable to stakeholders: '90% of alerts are real' (precision) and 'we catch 70% of cases' (recall).
Per-class and macro/weighted variants extend cleanly to multiclass and multilabel problems with explicit averaging choices.
disadvantages: All three depend on one threshold—comparing models fairly may require ROC-AUC or PR-AUC before picking an operating point.
Equal weight in F1 may not match business costs when false positives and false negatives have asymmetric dollar impact.
High precision with tiny recall (or vice versa) can still yield a misleadingly moderate F1 if only one side is reported.
Rare positive classes produce unstable precision estimates on small validation sets unless support is reported alongside.
